questions.question: Derive and explain Amdahl's Law formula for maximum parallel speedup. A program takes 100 seconds on a single processor with 30% being inherently serial. Calculate maximum speedup with unlimited processors.
keyPoints: Amdahl's Law: S ≤ 1/(1-P) where P = parallelizable fraction
Serial portion limits maximum speedup regardless of processor count
Given: Serial portion = 30% = 0.3, so P = 0.7
Maximum speedup = 1/(1-0.7) = 1/0.3 ≈ 3.33x
This demonstrates diminishing returns from adding more processors

questions.question: Compare and contrast Data Parallelism and Task Parallelism with suitable examples in cloud computing contexts.
keyPoints: Data Parallelism: Same operation on different data elements simultaneously
Task Parallelism: Different operations executing concurrently
Data parallelism examples: MapReduce map phase, GPU computing, vector operations
Task parallelism examples: Web server handling requests, pipeline stages, independent microservices
Trade-offs: Data parallelism simpler but requires regular data, task parallelism more flexible but complex scheduling

questions.question: Explain the three-tier client-server architecture and discuss its suitability for cloud deployment.
keyPoints: Presentation tier (UI), Application tier (business logic), Data tier (storage)
Separation enables independent scaling of tiers
Cloud suitability: Auto-scaling for presentation, stateless application servers, managed databases
Benefits: Maintainability, scalability, flexibility
Examples: Traditional web applications, enterprise systems

questions.question: Describe the Actor Model of computation. How does it handle concurrency and what are its advantages in distributed systems?
keyPoints: Actor: Independent entity with private state, processes messages sequentially
Communication via asynchronous message passing
Can create new actors, send messages to other actors
Inherent concurrency without locks or shared memory
Advantages: Fault tolerance, location transparency, scalable distribution
Used in Erlang, Akka framework for telecom, chat systems

questions.question: Calculate the speedup and efficiency for a program that runs in 200 seconds sequentially and 40 seconds on 8 processors. Comment on the results.
keyPoints: Speedup S = T(1)/T(P) = 200/40 = 5x
Efficiency E = S/P = 5/8 = 0.625 = 62.5%
Ideal speedup would be 8x with 100% efficiency
62.5% efficiency indicates overhead from communication, synchronization, or non-parallelizable sections
Remaining parallelizable portion: Using Amdahl: S = 1/(1-P) = 5, solving P = 0.8 or 80%

questions.question: Explain Event-Driven Architecture in cloud computing. What are its advantages and typical use cases?
keyPoints: Components communicate through events (state changes) rather than direct calls
Event producers emit to event bus/broker; consumers subscribe to relevant events
Advantages: Loose coupling, scalability, real-time responsiveness, resilience
Use cases: IoT data ingestion, real-time analytics, notification systems, stock trading platforms
Cloud services: AWS Lambda, Azure Event Grid, Kafka

questions.question: A cloud-based video processing system needs to process uploaded videos through: (1) Format conversion, (2) Thumbnail generation, (3) Quality optimization, (4) Metadata extraction. The processing times for each stage are 30s, 10s, 25s, and 5s respectively on a single processor. Design a pipeline architecture and calculate: (a) Total time to process 100 videos sequentially (b) Total time with pipeline parallelism (c) What is the bottleneck stage and how would you address it?
keyPoints: Pipeline with P stages: First video takes 70s (fill), remaining 99 complete at bottleneck rate (25s each) = 70 + 99×25 = 2575 seconds
Bottleneck: Stage 3 (quality optimization) at 25s—limits throughput
Solutions: Horizontal scaling (multiple processors for slow stage), stage optimization, parallelization within stage
Throughput: 1 video per 25 seconds after pipeline fills

questions.question: Discuss the Peer-to-Peer (P2P) architecture model in distributed systems. Compare it with client-server architecture, highlighting advantages, challenges, and applications in cloud environments.
keyPoints: P2P: Decentralized where each node acts as client and server, shares resources directly
Client-Server: Centralized server handles requests, clients are consumers
P2P Advantages: No single point of failure, scalable, cost-effective, resilient to load spikes
P2P Challenges: Security, consistency, discovery, free-riding, legal issues
Client-Server Advantages: Simpler, predictable, easier to secure and manage
P2P Applications: File sharing (BitTorrent), blockchain/ cryptocurrency, distributed storage (IPFS), collaborative apps
Hybrid approaches: Centralized index with P2P transfers (Napster model)

questions.question: Explain Microservices architecture pattern. How does it relate to cloud computing, and what are the challenges in implementing inter-service communication and data consistency?
keyPoints: Microservices: Decompose application into independently deployable services around business capabilities
Each service has its own database, communicates via lightweight APIs (REST, gRPC)
Cloud relationship: Native cloud pattern—each microservice can scale independently, deploy in containers (Docker, Kubernetes)
Communication challenges: Network latency, service discovery, fault tolerance, versioning
Data consistency: Each service owns data—challenges in maintaining consistency across services
Solutions: Event-driven (eventual consistency), saga pattern, CQRS, distributed transactions
Advantages: Technology heterogeneity, independent deployment, scalability, team autonomy
Challenges: Complexity in operations, debugging, testing, data duplication
